title: Ginger, herb profile
subtitle: The kitchen's most useful medicinal herb
excerpt: A short reference profile of ginger root: traditional uses, ways to prepare it, the usual doses for tea, compress, and bath, and the safety notes that matter. Present in every kitchen and one of the most studied herbs.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), ginger entry. King's American Dispensatory (Felter and Lloyd, 1898), ginger entry. WHO Monograph on selected medicinal plants (1999), Zingiber officinale entry, traditional use for nausea and digestive complaints. EMA HMPC monograph on Zingiberis rhizoma (2012), traditional herbal medicinal product for nausea and dyspeptic complaints.

content.text: Ginger is the root of a tropical grass grown across South and Southeast Asia, India, China, and West Africa. In British kitchens it shows up as fresh root, dried ground spice, crystallised ginger, and extract. In herbal medicine it is used fresh, dried, or as a tincture. Fresh and dried are not the same in strength: fresh ginger is richer in
, while drying turns some of those into shogaols, which are warmer and stronger. Cooking ginger and medicinal ginger are the same plant. The dose makes the difference.

Ginger has been used at home for centuries to settle the stomach, ease wind, warm cold hands and feet, calm gripey gut, bring on a sweat at the start of a cold, and loosen a tight chest. Older herbals classed it as a stimulant, a carminative for digestion, and a warming, circulation-rousing herb.
What it is used for today

Nausea of most kinds: travel sickness, morning sickness at low dose, post-operative nausea, and as a side-by-side support for nausea after chemotherapy. Digestive complaints: bloating, wind, and indigestion. Aching muscles and cold hands and feet: the ginger bath and compress. The shivery first stage of a cold: the warming bath and a hot ginger drink.

Ginger is one of the most studied herbs. Many trials and reviews back its use for nausea, including pregnancy nausea at low dose. European regulators list it as a traditional herbal product for nausea and digestive complaints. The evidence for aching muscles is thinner but the home use is long-standing.

Hot infusion: 10 g fresh ginger (a 2 cm piece, sliced thin) per 250 ml water, steeped covered for 10 minutes. That gives about 1 g of dried-ginger strength, the lower end of the working dose. Up to three cups a day for short bouts of nausea.

Compress: 30 g fresh ginger simmered in 500 ml water for 15 minutes, applied hot to aching muscles. Bath: 100 g fresh ginger simmered in 1 litre water, then poured into a warm bath. Tincture: 1:5 in 40% spirit, 2 to 4 ml up to three times a day.

Cooking doses and a cup or two of fresh ginger tea a day suit most adults. Ginger can thin the blood a little, so anyone on warfarin, aspirin, or clopidogrel should run regular daily use past the prescriber. In pregnancy, cooking use is fine and up to 1 g of dried-ginger strength a day for morning sickness is widely supported; bigger doses are less well studied and best discussed with a midwife. Used on the skin, ginger can redden or sting sensitive skin, so test on a small patch before a full bath or compress.

attrs.title: Medical note
attrs.body: Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.
